input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is the serial robot MABI MAX 100, an industrial six‑axis robot used in manufacturing. Axis 1 is driven by a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor that is coupled to a cycloidal gear; the remaining axes have similar drive arrangements. The robot is controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl CNC controller, providing real‑time motion execution and monitoring. Its design targets high‑precision, multi‑axis positioning tasks in assembly and material handling.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides a visualization dashboard for real‑time monitoring of robot states, supports continuous multi‑physics‑aware identification of joint friction (monitoring and diagnostic service), and offers motion‑accuracy enhancement through model‑based correction (optimization/prediction service). These services enable operators to analyse performance, predict deviations, and adjust control parameters to improve precision.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on MathWorks MATLAB/Simulink for dynamic simulation of robot kinematics and friction models, SysML for structural and behavioral modeling, UML class diagrams for the digital‑shadow reference model, Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized data representation, MQTT for low‑latency data streaming between the physical robot and the twin, and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller as the execution platform. Supporting tools include databases for storing data traces and processing components that execute simulation and data‑calculation models.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin follows the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM), where a Processing component consumes input DataTraces, executes engineering, simulation, or data‑calculation Models, and produces output DataTraces. The 'originates‑from' association links each DataTrace back to the generating Processing component, its consumed input traces, and the models involved, maintaining transitive traceability. This modular arrangement of Processing components and Models constitutes the system‑wide orchestration and coordination of the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin relies on a hierarchy of computational models defined through Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE). Structural and behavioral aspects are captured with SysML (including internal block diagrams, state machines) and architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, or MontiArc. Simulation models are implemented in MathWorks Simulink for white‑box (physics‑based) dynamics, while black‑box components employ machine‑learning models (e.g., neural networks) to learn non‑linear friction behaviour. Data traces originating from the physical robot are ingested by a Processing component that consumes raw sensor streams, applies the simulation or data‑calculation models, and produces derived output traces that become part of the digital shadow. Model relationships are traced via the 'originates from' association, preserving transitive provenance from input traces through processing components to output data. The Twin stores all model artefacts and generated data in a central repository (e.g., Thingsboard) enabling reuse by other applications.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is maintained by continuous verification of both white‑box Simulink simulations and black‑box learning models against incoming sensor data. The approach emphasizes traceability of transformations (e.g., SysML to simulation code) and verification of model correctness as described in the literature. Uncertainty is addressed through dynamic adaptation: model parameters are automatically updated and neural networks are retrained as new data arrive, mitigating drift due to wear, temperature, or payload changes. Quality assurance includes transitive provenance tracking of data traces, enabling error detection and correction, and supports predictive maintenance by detecting anomalies in friction behaviour.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The system implements an automated one‑way data flow from the physical asset to its digital counterpart, as defined for a digital shadow. Sensor measurements are collected as time‑series traces on the physical object and transmitted upstream via the MQTT publish‑subscribe protocol. The flow is unidirectional; incoming sensor streams are aggregated and abstracted by processing components that map raw traces to higher‑level models within the digital shadow reference model. No explicit transmission frequency, event‑trigger rules, or data‑quality assurance procedures are detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between the physical sensors and the digital shadow relies on the MQTT protocol, a lightweight publish‑subscribe messaging standard. The source documents only specify the use of MQTT; additional network characteristics such as topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security mechanisms are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering of a Digital Twin follows an MBSE‑driven workflow described in the documents. System structure and behaviour are captured across multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product) using SysML block diagrams and internal block diagrams. Component‑connector architecture is modelled with ADLs such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc or SysML IBDs, describing components, interfaces, interconnections and composition. Behavioural aspects are specified with SysML state machines (v1.7) or external simulation tools (e.g., Matlab/Simulink) and may be refined by source code in C++ or Java. A Digital Shadow Reference Model is instantiated by a concretization step: concrete classes and relations are mapped to the reference concepts, producing a conforming model. Processing components are defined that consume input data traces, execute data‑calculation or simulation models, and generate output traces that become part of the shadow. Traceability is preserved through the ‘originates‑from’ association, enabling transitive back‑tracking from any data trace to its generating processing component, input traces and involved models. Evolution management is achieved by updating the concrete realization and its mapping to the reference model while retaining traceability links, thereby supporting versioning of processing logic, simulation or calculation models.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Within the DT, processing components run engineering, simulation, or data‑calculation models on incoming data traces and emit derived data that constitute higher‑level abstractions. These derived traces can be visualized in dashboards or consumed by downstream analytics pipelines, providing operators with actionable insights such as performance deviations or predicted maintenance needs. The fine‑grained traceability (via the originates‑from links) enables users to assess the confidence of each insight by examining the lineage of input traces, models, and processing steps that produced it.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The DT leverages several established standards: SysML is used for systems modeling; UML class diagrams describe the Digital Shadow Reference Model; ADLs such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc provide component‑connector specifications; and the notion of a conformance relation follows the formal definition of reference models. By instantiating the reference model with concrete classes that map to these standards, the DT ensures interoperability and alignment with industry modeling practices.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[62] Z. Huang, Y. Shen, J. Li, M. Fey, and C. Brecher, “A survey on aidriven digital twins in industry 4.0: Smart manufacturing and advanced
robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)
[12-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

[66] H. M. Muctadir, D. A. Manrique Negrin, R. Gunasekaran, L. Cleophas,
M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct
[2024. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z)

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

However, the score is reduced because it claims the remaining axes have similar drive arrangements (which is not explicitly stated in the text) and lists 'assembly and material handling' as tasks, while the source text specifically mentions 'machining'.

The extraction is highly specific and directly grounded in the provided text. It accurately captures the services described in the abstract and introduction, including the 'visualization in a dashboard', 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', and the use of data to 'enhance the robot’s motion accuracy'.

Provides a comprehensive and accurate list of the tools and technologies mentioned in the source. It correctly identifies MathWorks MATLAB/Simulink for simulation, SysML/UML for modeling, Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for data representation, MQTT for data streaming, and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl as the execution platform.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions general temporal concepts like 'real-time' and 'live data during the operation,' but it does not provide the specific technical details required by the characteristic description, such as 'synchronization frequencies,' 'latency requirements,' or specific 'temporal granularity needs.' The claim 'Not in Document' is correct regarding these specific details.

The document focuses on a single digital shadow instance for a specific robot axis. It explicitly states 'FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow' and does not discuss multiple instances, hierarchical structures, or coordination mechanisms between twins. The claim 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction accurately summarizes the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) found in the source. It correctly identifies the Processing component that 'consumes input DataTraces, executes... Models, and produces output DataTraces', which is supported by the text: 'explicit integration of the Processing component, which both consumes and produces data traces and processes data calculation and simulation models'. It also correctly describes the traceability mechanism: 'By following the originates from association, one can trace a data trace back to its generating processing component...'

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source focuses on the internal modeling of the robot (SysML, Simulink) and the internal data flow of the digital shadow (MQTT). It does not discuss integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards for cross-system communication, or dependencies on systems outside of the specific robot setup described. Thus, the relevant information is missing.

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],

and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.

behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

**Fig. 8:** Sequence diagram of the AAS Executor for populating measured, simulated, and reference values to the digital
shadow stored in Thingsboard.


of friction characteristics as operating conditions, loads, and
environmental factors change. This continuous data stream allows for the dynamic adaptation of both physics-based (whitebox) and data-driven (black-box) friction models, ensuring
that the models remain accurate and relevant throughout the
robot’s operational life. Such adaptability is crucial for highprecision applications, where even minor deviations in friction
behavior can significantly impact positioning accuracy and
process quality. With digital shadows, it becomes possible
to automatically update model parameters or retrain neural
network-based models as new data becomes available, compensating for wear, temperature changes, or varying payloads.
This results in more robust and reliable friction compensation
strategies, reducing trajectory errors and enhancing the overall
performance of robotic systems. Moreover, the integration of
digital shadows into the robot’s control architecture supports
predictive maintenance and early fault detection, as anomalies
in friction behavior can be detected and addressed proactively.
Ultimately, digital shadows transform friction modeling from
a static, one-time process into a dynamic, continuously improving capability that drives higher accuracy and reliability
in industrial robotics.

Based on this paper, a digital twin of the robot can be
developed to improve machining processes. Here, particular
attention must be paid to the real-time requirements of the calculations and the associated dead time of the control system.
By implementing the friction models in the twin, we can identify the internal joint friction that occurs during runtime, which
can be compensated for by the control system. The resulting
increased accuracy of the robot benefits every application. If,
in addition to the internal friction, compensation of the external



forces caused by the machining process is implemented in
the friction model, simulative experiments can be carried out.
This makes it possible to optimize the machining parameters
and identify critical sub-processes before the start of the
real process. Once a digital twin has been set up with its
communication and data storage components, it serves as a
central data memory. It can therefore be easily supplemented
by other applications, thus accelerating their development
process. In the present application case, for example, the
cutting parameters can be optimized to reduce _CO_ 2 or a
product quality prediction can be developed based on the
production data.


VI. D ISCUSSION AND R ELATED W ORK


_A. Strengths and Limitations of the Approach_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:
